tweets.text: RT @MarioNawfal: 🚨🇺🇸Massie and Khanna file a follow-up to their Epstein law, letting states and survivors SUE the Attorney General over 3 million withheld files. -The Epstein Files Transparency Act II would give state attorneys general and survivors standing to sue the U.S. Attorney General to force compliance with the original law Trump signed -Massie's charge: the DOJ "continues to unlawfully withhold over 3 million Epstein files that should be released to the public" and is "unlawfully maintaining heavy redactions on the files it is releasing" -Khanna says the department "violated our law, delayed the release of millions of files, botched the redactions, and denied the survivors justice" -The specific trigger: New Mexico's attorney general is ready to prosecute crimes at Zorro Ranch but cannot get DOJ cooperation, so the bill routes documents straight to any state AG willing to bring charges -The DOJ's July 2 filing pushed back, with Acting AG Todd Blanche arguing further release would harm victims and the government, offering instead to share materials "in camera," meaning behind closed doors with a judge The workaround is the whole design. Massie believes the DOJ has no interest in prosecuting the men the records expose, so the fix is to hand the files to prosecutors who do. Khanna's answer for why this issue refuses to die is worth sitting with, that people are watching rich and powerful men accused of trafficking girls walk away clean and drawing conclusions about the country. Source: @RepThomasMassie @RoKhanna CNN / Writer: Daniel
tweets.summary: RT @MarioNawfal: 🚨🇺🇸Massie and Khanna file a follow-up to their Epstein law, letting states and survivors SUE the Attorney General over 3 million…

tweets.text: RT @RepMeuser: Today, our @FinancialCmte Subcommittee on Oversight and Investigations released our report titled “Fighting Back: A Policy Framework for Combating the Rise of Financial Fraud & Scams”. The report details the extent of financial fraud and scams facing the American public, explains what’s being done by the public and private sectors, and identifies policy solutions to address this growing problem. Financial Services Committee Chairman @RepFrenchHill and I participated in a fireside chat detailing the report’s findings and recommendations for combating cyber criminals and highlighting the insidiousness of scam networks, often coming from overseas. Thank you, @LACaldwellDC of @pucknews, for moderating this important event.

tweets.text: RT @SpeakerJohnson: My joint statement with Leader @SteveScalise, @GOPMajorityWhip Emmer, and @HouseGOP Chairwoman @RepLisaMcClain on the House passage of the Budget Resolution for Reconciliation 3.0 — the SAVE and Protect America Act: Today, House Republicans passed the Budget Resolution for Reconciliation 3.0 — the SAVE and Protect America Act — which puts Congressional Republicans on a path to enacting two fundamentally simple and crucial goals: securing American elections and strengthening our homeland by providing supplemental funding requested by the Administration to support our national defense and protect our nation’s food supply. After the Senate passes this Budget Resolution, our House Committees will begin the process of drafting and passing the SAVE and Protect America Act. This Budget Resolution is our best shot at enacting as much of the SAVE America Act into law as possible. This is a bill that every Republican in Congress can and should get behind.

tweets.text: Democrat opposition to the historic ethics provision in the Clarity Act appears to take one of two forms, either: (1) An ethics provision that lacks enforcement by state AGs is meaningless. (2) An ethics provision that does not penalize President Trump for prior crypto activity is unsatisfactory. If you hold position (1), then you are basically saying that ALL current federal ethics laws are meaningless because none of them are enforceable by state AGs. If you hold position (2), then there is literally nothing that can be done to appease you because what you are advocating for is blatantly unconstitutional (see Article I, Section 9).

tweets.text: RT @brian_armstrong: The Clarity Act is ready for a full Senate floor vote. The bill represents a true bipartisan compromise with thousands of hours of work on both sides, and it couldn't come at a better time. The status quo in the U.S. isn't working. There's no federal framework, so bad actors like FTX can harm U.S. customers and much of the industry has gone offshore totally outside U.S. purview. This bill fixes that with strong consumer protections, real tools for law enforcement, and a path for America to lead in this industry. Crypto can't be uninvented at this point, so whether you love crypto or hate crypto, you should want clear federal laws. And it's also simply good politics: 70% of American voters say the U.S. should have already passed comprehensive crypto legislation. Let's finish this! Special thanks to the @standwithcrypto members who sent 950k contacts to their representatives in congress. Keep it up.
